description: Companion to trials.json: notable new ME/CFS research papers (pathomechanism, biomarkers, drug targets, treatment/trial results, genetics), prioritising Wirth/Scheibenbogen but NOT Germany-restricted; recent (~last 3 months / since last_check). Shown on the dashboard's Papers tab. Confirm each via DOI/journal/preprint before recording; never fabricate. STYLE: write 'summary' and 'why' for a layperson — plain language, short sentences, jargon only where unavoidable and explained in a few words.

papers.title: Imbalance of Excitatory and Inhibitory Neurotransmitter Systems in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.summary: Reviews evidence that ME/CFS disturbs the brain's chemical messengers — the signals nerve cells use to talk to each other — which may explain symptoms like brain fog and feeling 'wired but tired'.
papers.why: From the two leading German ME/CFS researchers: it links the disease's effects in the muscles to its effects in the brain, reinforcing the case for the treatments they are developing.
papers.notes: PMID 42123618. Published April 30, 2026. Int J Mol Sci 27(9):4041.

papers.title: Charting the circulating proteome in ME/CFS using cross-system profiling to uncover mechanistic insights
papers.authors: Hoel A, Hoel F, Dyrstad SE, Chapola H, Rekeland IG, Risa K, Alme K, Sørland K, Brokstad KA, Marti HP, Mella O, Fluge Ø, Tronstad KJ
papers.summary: Measured thousands of proteins in patients' blood and found many that differ from healthy people — signs of an overactive immune system and poor blood flow to the muscles.
papers.why: The largest blood-protein study in ME/CFS so far; it maps what is going wrong in the body and could lead to a blood test and new treatment targets.
papers.notes: Published March 4, 2026. From the Fluge/Mella group (Bergen, Norway).

papers.title: Proteomic signatures in cerebrospinal fluid and their clinical associations in patients with ME/CFS
papers.authors: Bragée B, Li P, Meadows D, et al.
papers.summary: Examined proteins in the fluid that bathes the brain and spinal cord, and found inflammation-related patterns that matched how severe patients were and whether they also had a racing heart on standing up (a condition called POTS).
papers.why: One of the first studies of this fluid in ME/CFS; the patterns it found could become markers for sorting patients into the right treatment groups.

papers.title: Pathophysiological mechanisms of post-exertional malaise: an integrative analysis based on the metabolism-immune-neuro interaction model
papers.authors: Jin H, An Y, Huang J, Luo T, Wu X
papers.summary: Proposes how even light activity can trigger a 'crash' (the delayed worsening of symptoms after exertion, called post-exertional malaise): the cells' energy units falter, this sparks inflammation, and the inflammation reaches the brain and keeps the cycle going.
papers.why: Offers a clear, testable explanation for ME/CFS's defining symptom — crashing after exertion — and points toward energy- and inflammation-based treatments.
papers.notes: Published April 13, 2026. Frontiers in Immunology vol. 17.

papers.title: Elevated serum levels of interleukin-11 and matrix metalloproteinase-9 in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Chinnappan B, Kempuraj D, Aenlle KK, Middleton A, Day KS, Kothuru SP, Joshi RS, Klimas NG, Theoharides TC
papers.summary: Found two inflammation-related substances raised in patients' blood, and linked them to immune cells reacting to the Epstein–Barr virus (the cause of glandular fever).
papers.why: These substances could serve as a blood marker, and the link to a common virus hints at new treatment ideas.

papers.title: Large-scale investigation confirms TRPM3 ion channel dysfunction in Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Sasso EM, Er TS, Eaton-Fitch N, Hool L, Muraki K, Marshall-Gradisnik S
papers.summary: Confirmed, across several laboratories, that a tiny 'gate' which lets calcium into certain immune cells doesn't work properly in ME/CFS patients.
papers.why: Because independent labs got the same result, this faulty cell 'gate' is one of the most reliable biological signs of ME/CFS and a possible drug target.
papers.notes: Published January 8, 2026. Frontiers in Medicine vol. 12.

papers.title: Plasma cell targeting with the anti-CD38 antibody daratumumab in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome — a clinical pilot study
papers.authors: Fluge Ø, Rekeland IG, Sørland K, Alme K, Risa K, Bruland O, Tronstad KJ, Mella O
papers.summary: A small trial gave 10 patients daratumumab — a drug that clears the immune cells which make antibodies; 6 of 10 improved and several stayed better for one to two years.
papers.why: The first trial of this approach in ME/CFS; the strong, lasting improvement supports the idea that misguided antibodies drive the illness, and a larger trial is planned.
papers.notes: Published July 9, 2025. Fluge/Mella group, Haukeland University Hospital, Bergen, Norway.

papers.title: Safety, tolerability and clinical effects of rovunaptabin (BC007) on fatigue and quality of life in patients with Post-COVID syndrome (reCOVer): a phase IIa randomised controlled trial
papers.authors: Hohberger B, Ganslmayer M, Harrer T, et al.
papers.summary: In a rigorous trial with a placebo group, a drug that neutralises harmful 'self-attacking' antibodies reduced fatigue and improved quality of life in Long COVID patients.
papers.why: The first properly controlled trial showing benefit from removing these antibodies — strong support for antibody-targeting treatments that are also being explored in ME/CFS.
papers.notes: Published July 22, 2025. University Hospital Erlangen, Germany. This is the reCOVer trial result.

papers.title: Key Pathophysiological Role of Skeletal Muscle Disturbance in Post COVID and Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS): Accumulated Evidence
papers.journal: Journal of Cachexia, Sarcopenia and Muscle
papers.summary: Pulls together evidence that ME/CFS muscles get too little blood and build up salt (sodium) and calcium, which damages the muscle cells' energy units.
papers.why: A cornerstone of the Wirth/Scheibenbogen theory and the direct rationale for their drug MDC002, which aims to fix exactly this muscle problem.
papers.notes: Published February 2025. PMID 39727052. J Cachexia Sarcopenia Muscle 16(1). Wirth + Scheibenbogen joint review — HIGH PRIORITY.

papers.title: Expert perspectives on myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome — Insights from the 3rd International Conference of the Charité Fatigue Center
papers.summary: Summarises what experts at Charité's international ME/CFS conference agree on — problems with circulation, the immune system, and the body's energy use — and warns that steady research funding is urgently needed.
papers.why: Lays out the Scheibenbogen group's current priorities and the thinking behind their trials (immunoadsorption and the planned inebilizumab study).
papers.notes: PMID 41895458. Lead author Annick Fehrer, Charité Berlin. Epub March 25 2026. Confirmed via Elsevier.

papers.title: Tryptophan Metabolism and Aryl-Hydrocarbon Receptor Agonists in the Gut Microbiome of People With Myalgic Encephalomyelitis/Chronic Fatigue Syndrome
papers.authors: Esteban DJ, Conrad B, Cullinan A, et al.
papers.summary: Found that the gut bacteria of ME/CFS patients handle the nutrient tryptophan differently, making fewer of the molecules that help keep the immune system calm.
papers.why: Points to the gut as a possible lever on the immune system, supporting microbiome- and immune-based treatment ideas.

papers.title: Systems neuroendocrinology in ME/CFS and long COVID: a chronobiological framework for hormone-based research
papers.authors: Thomas N, Huang K, Schneider-Futschik EK, et al.
papers.summary: Argues that hormone studies in ME/CFS and Long COVID disagree because they ignore differences by time of day and menstrual-cycle phase, and recommends accounting for these.
papers.why: Better-designed hormone studies could uncover treatment targets; gives practical guidance for trials, especially those with mostly female participants.
papers.notes: PMID 42320559. Front Neuroendocrinol. Confirmed via PubMed.

papers.title: Gastrointestinal symptoms correlate with core clinical features and systemic inflammation in myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome
papers.authors: Brown M, Vernon SD, Indart AC, Green PH, Alaedini A
papers.summary: Showed that the worse a patient's gut symptoms are, the worse their main ME/CFS symptoms and body-wide inflammation tend to be — suggesting the gut is part of the disease, not a side issue.
papers.why: Supports treating gut problems as central to ME/CFS, which could shape who is enrolled in drug trials and how improvement is measured.
papers.notes: PMID 42321833. J Transl Med. Confirmed via PubMed.
